note_data.title: Particle physics
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated using ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object moving in circular motion at constant speed has constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, therefore it must be accelerating
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to the motion towards the centre of the circle
Centripetal acceleration is given by a = v²/r = ω²r
Centripetal force is calculated using F = mv²/r = mω²r
A gravitational field is an area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
Newton's law of gravitation states F = Gm₁m₂/r², where force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
Gravitational field strength in a radial field follows g = GM/r², showing an inverse square relationship

definitions.definition: The angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The force that acts perpendicular to the motion of an object moving in a circle, always directed towards the centre of the circle
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State clear differences between two particle types with examples
Demonstrate conservation laws are satisfied by checking each quantum number

modelAnswer.question: A neutron decays into a proton, an electron and an electron antineutrino. Show that charge and baryon number are conserved in this decay. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Charge conservation: neutron (0) → proton (+1) + electron (-1) + antineutrino (0). Total charge: 0 = +1 + (-1) + 0 = 0 ✓. Baryon number conservation: neutron (+1) → proton (+1) + electron (0) + antineutrino (0). Total baryon number: +1 = +1 + 0 + 0 = +1 ✓. Both charge and baryon number are conserved ✓.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct baryon number calculation showing +1 = +1
Mark 3: Statement that both are conserved

commonMistakes: Forgetting to convert degrees to radians by multiplying by π/180
Thinking that constant speed in circular motion means no acceleration - velocity is constantly changing due to changing direction
Confusing the direction of centripetal force - it always acts towards the centre, not away from it
Not recognizing that gravitational field strength follows an inverse square relationship with distance
examinerTips: Remember that centripetal acceleration and force have two equivalent forms: a = v²/r = ω²r and F = mv²/r = mω²r
When calculating gravitational forces, treat uniform spheres as point masses at their centres for objects outside the sphere
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Standard Model chart showing three generations of quarks and leptons, with their charges and masses, plus the four fundamental forces and their gauge bosons
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe the structure of an atom in terms of protons, neutrons and electrons, and explain how isotopes differ from each other
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which particle has the smallest mass (electron, proton, neutron, alpha particle)
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students had to explain radioactive decay using particle physics concepts and identify the particles emitted in alpha and beta decay
N/D 2021 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — question required explanation of how cloud chamber or bubble chamber tracks show different types of radiation based on particle properties
M/J 2021 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — asked students to identify which statement about atomic structure is correct, testing knowledge of relative masses and charges

workedExample.question: An atom of carbon-14 undergoes beta-minus decay. State the particles present in the nucleus before decay, describe what happens during the decay process, and identify the new element formed. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify initial nucleus composition - Carbon-14 has mass number 14, atomic number 6, so contains 6 protons and 8 neutrons
Step 2: Describe beta-minus decay process - A neutron in the nucleus converts to a proton, releasing an electron (beta particle) and an antineutrino
Step 3: Apply conservation laws - Mass number stays 14, but atomic number increases from 6 to 7
Step 4: Identify new element - Atomic number 7 corresponds to nitrogen, so carbon-14 becomes nitrogen-14
workedExample.answer: Initially: 6 protons and 8 neutrons in the nucleus. During beta-minus decay, one neutron converts to a proton, emitting an electron (beta particle) and an antineutrino. The mass number remains 14 but the atomic number increases to 7. The new element formed is nitrogen-14.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct initial composition, 2 marks for describing neutron-to-proton conversion and particle emission, 1 mark for conservation of mass number, 1 mark for increase in atomic number, 1 mark for identifying nitrogen

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused mass and charge when describing fundamental particles' — always state both the relative mass AND relative charge for protons, neutrons and electrons
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often wrote that electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed paths like planets' — remember that electrons exist in probability clouds or energy levels, not definite orbits
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was stating that neutrons have no effect on atomic mass' — emphasize that neutrons contribute significantly to atomic mass despite having no charge
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a simple atomic structure diagram showing: nucleus at center (labeled with protons and neutrons), electron shells/energy levels as circles around nucleus (not as orbital paths), correct relative sizes (nucleus much smaller than atom), and proper labeling of all particles with their charges (+1 for protons, 0 for neutrons, -1 for electrons). Include mass numbers and atomic numbers for specific examples.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement measured in radians and angular speed related to linear speed by ω = v/r. Objects in circular motion experience centripetal acceleration towards the centre, requiring centripetal force. Gravitational fields exert forces on masses according to Newton's law of gravitation, with field strength following an inverse square relationship with distance.
